result.markdown:
# Wiener processes Here we review stochastic process that are useful in finance, especially in the Black-Scholes context. This section is based on @Hull, chapter 14. ## The basic process A simple Wiener process. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # A typical Wiener process. TT <- 5 N <- 10000 dt <- TT / N Time <- seq(from = dt, to = TT, by = dt) set.seed(560746) # for reproducibility purposes. delta_z <- rnorm(N, 0, 1) * dt^0.5 z <- 25 + cumsum(delta_z) # The initial price is 25. ``` ::: Graphically: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(Time, z, type = "l", ylab = "z in the texbook, it could be a stock price", xlab = "Time") lines(Time, seq(25, (25 + sum(delta_z)), length.out = N), lty = 2, col = "purple") points(Time[1], 25, col = "blue", cex = 2, pch = 16) points(Time[N], z[N], col = "red", cex = 2, pch = 16) legend("topleft", legend = c(round(z[N], 2)), bg = "white", text.col = "red") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Wiener process 10,000 steps from blue $z(0)=25$ to $z(T)=26.2$ in red.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-wp1sfbtir-1.png){#fig-wp1sfbtir width=672} ::: ::: This is a 1,000 step version of four Wiener processes: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} embed_url("https://youtu.be/X-VRWeqG_I8") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This is supposed to mimic (at some extent) the evolution of a stock price. Now, we propose to produce 100 processes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # 100 Wiener processes. set.seed(365633) mat.delta_z <- matrix(rnorm(100 * N, mean = 0, sd = 1) * sqrt(dt), 100, N) mat.z1 <- 25 + t(apply(mat.delta_z, 1, cumsum)) mat.z <- t(cbind(matrix(25, 100), mat.z1)) Time2 <- c(0, Time) pred <- mean(mat.z[(N + 1), ]) # 95% range. rangetop <- pred + qnorm(0.975) * var(mat.z[(N + 1), ])^0.5 rangedown <- pred - qnorm(0.975) * var(mat.z[(N + 1), ])^0.5 ``` ::: Graphically. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} matplot(Time2, mat.z, type = "l", lty = 1, col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.3), ylab = "z in the textbook, it could be a stock price", xlab = "Time") abline(h = pred, lty = 2, col = "red") abline(v = 0, lty = 2) lines(Time2, seq(25, pred, length.out = N + 1), lty = 2) points(Time2[N + 1], pred, col = "red", cex = 2, pch = 16) points(0, 25, col = "black", cex = 2, pch = 16) legend("topleft", legend = c(round(pred, 2)), bg = "white", text.col = "red") abline(h = rangetop, lty = 2) abline(h = rangedown, lty = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![100 Wiener process 10,000 steps, initial price at 25, red is the mean at $T$.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-owp1sipa2ritmat-1.png){#fig-owp1sipa2ritmat width=672} ::: ::: Let's check how many simulated terminal values fall outside this 95% prediction range. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sum(mat.z[(N + 1), ] > rangetop) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} sum(mat.z[(N + 1), ] < rangedown) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2 ``` ::: ::: This means that 5 paths are outside this simulated 95% prediction range. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(mat.z[(N + 1), ]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Min. 1st Qu. Median Mean 3rd Qu. Max. 20.97 23.79 24.96 25.02 26.06 31.38 ``` ::: ::: A potential problem is that $z$ values can be negative. This is problematic when we are interested to model stock prices. Let's explore deeper the stochastic process below. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(1) delta_z <- rnorm(N, 0, 1) * dt^0.5 z <- 25 + cumsum(delta_z) ### A stochastic process par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mai = c(0.4, 0.4, 0.4, 0.4)) par(pty = "s") plot(Time, z, type = "l", xlim = c(0, TT), ylim = c(22.5, 26), main = "Full 5-year period", ylab = "z", xlab = "Time") plot(Time, delta_z, type = "h", xlim = c(0, TT), main = "Full 5-year period", ylab = expression(paste(Delta,z)), xlab = "Time") abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "orange", lwd = 2) plot(Time, z, type = "l", xlim = c(0, 1/12), ylim = c(24.9, 25.35), main = "Zoom: first month only", xlab = "Time") plot(Time, delta_z, type = "h", xlim = c(0, 1/12), col = ifelse(delta_z < 0, "red", "blue"), ylim = c(-0.055, 0.055), main = "Zoom: first month only", ylab = expression(paste(Delta,z)), xlab = "Time") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A stochastic process with zoom.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-aspwz-1.png){#fig-aspwz width=672} ::: ::: These processes are driven by a random component. The lower panel is revealing because we can see how the random component goes from positive to negative without any pattern, it is entirely random. Now, let's explore the role of $\Delta t$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mai = c(0.4, 0.4, 0.4, 0.4)) par(pty = "s") plot(Time[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 10))], z[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 10))], type = "l", ylim = c(22.5, 26), main = expression(paste("Large ",Delta,t)), ylab = "z") abline(v = 0, lty = 2) abline(v = 5, lty = 2) plot(Time[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 100))], z[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 100))], type = "l", ylim = c(22.5, 26), ylab = "z", main = expression(paste("Small ",Delta,t))) abline(v = 0, lty = 2) abline(v = 5 , lty = 2) plot(Time[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 1000))], z[(seq(from = 1, to = N, length.out = 1000))], type = "l", ylim = c(22.5, 26), ylab = "z", main = expression(paste("Even smaller ",Delta,t))) abline(v = 0, lty = 2) abline(v = 5 , lty = 2) plot(Time, z, type = "l", ylim = c(22.5, 26), # length 10,000 main = expression(paste("True process ",Delta,t, " ", "tends ","to ","zero"))) abline(v = 0, lty = 2) abline(v = 5, lty = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Wiener process, the role of $\Delta t$.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-wptrod-1.png){#fig-wptrod width=672} ::: ::: The $\Delta t$ value determines how frequent are the changes in $z$. A few properties of stochastic processes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mean(delta_z) # should be zero ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] -0.0001461726 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} var(delta_z)^0.5 # should be dt^.5 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.02263698 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt^0.5 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.02236068 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} var(delta_z) # should be dt ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.0005124328 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5e-04 ``` ::: ::: ## The generalized Wiener process Now let's analyze the case of the generalized Wiener process. $\Delta x = a \Delta t + b \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t}$. Assume $a=0.3$ and $b=1.5$: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Figure 14.2 TT <- 50 N <- 200 dt <- TT / N Time <- seq(from = 0, to = TT, by = dt) set.seed(123) delta_z <- 1.5 * rnorm(N, 0, 1) * dt^0.5 delta_x <- (0.3 * dt) + delta_z x <- c(0, cumsum(delta_x)) z <- c(0, cumsum(delta_z)) ``` ::: Graphically: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(Time, x, type = "l", lwd = 4, ylim = c(-7, 22)) lines(Time, z, col = "red", lwd = 4) lines(Time, 0.3 * Time, col = "blue", lwd = 4) abline(0, 0) abline(v = 0) legend("topleft", legend = c("Generalized Wiener process", "Drift", "Stochastic component"), col = c("black", "blue", "red"), lwd = 3, bg = "white", cex = 0.8) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Generalized Wiener process.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-gwp-1.png){#fig-gwp width=672} ::: ::: Here, the generalized Wiener process $\Delta x$ is decomposed into the drift $a \Delta t$ and the stochastic component $b \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t}$. A zoom of the same plot. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Figure 14.2 Zoom plot(Time, x, type = "b", ylim = c(-0.6, 1), xlim = c(0, 1), lwd = 4) lines(Time, z, col = "red", lwd = 4, type = "b") lines(Time, 0.3 * Time, col = "blue", lwd = 4, type = "b") abline(0, 0) abline(v = 0) abline(v = dt, lty = 2) abline(v = dt * 2, lty = 2) abline(v = dt * 3, lty = 2) abline(v = dt * 4, lty = 2) #points(dt, 0, pch = 1, col = "blue", lwd = 2) #points(dt * 2, 0, pch = 1, col = "blue", lwd = 2) #points(dt * 3, 0, pch = 1, col = "blue", lwd = 2) #points(dt * 4, 0, pch = 1, col = "blue", lwd = 2) legend("topleft", legend = c("Generalized Wiener process", "Drift", "Stochastic component"), col = c("black", "blue", "red"), lty = 1, bg = "white", lwd = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Generalized Wiener process, zoom view.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-gwpzv-1.png){#fig-gwpzv width=672} ::: ::: This is a nice visual representation of: generalized = drift + Wiener. See how the process changes when we consider a lower value of $a$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(123) delta_xlow <- (0.15 * dt) + delta_z xlow <- c(0, cumsum(delta_xlow)) # Now plot. plot(Time, xlow, type = "l", ylim = c(-7, 22), lwd = 4, ylab = "x") lines(Time, x, lwd = 2, col = "grey") lines(Time, z, lwd = 4, col = "red") lines(Time, 0.15 * Time, lwd = 4, col = "blue") lines(Time, 0.3 * Time, lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "grey") abline(0, 0) abline(v = 0) legend("topleft", legend = c("Original generalized Wiener process", "Original drift", "New generalized Wiener process", "New drift", "Stochastic component"), lty = c(1, 2, 1, 1, 1), bg = "white", lwd = 2, col = c("grey", "grey", "black", "blue", "red"), cex = 0.85) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Generalized Wiener process, lower a.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-gwpla-1.png){#fig-gwpla width=672} ::: ::: And a higher value of $b$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(123) delta_zhigh <- 3 * rnorm(N, 0, 1) * dt^0.5 delta_xhigh <- (0.3 * dt) + delta_zhigh xhigh <- c(0, cumsum(delta_xhigh)) zhigh <- c(0, cumsum(delta_zhigh)) plot(Time, xhigh, type = "l", ylim = c(-7, 22), lwd = 4, ylab = "x") lines(Time, x, lwd = 2, col = "grey") lines(Time, zhigh, lwd = 4, col = "red") lines(Time, z, lwd = 2, col = "grey") lines(Time, 0.3 * Time, lwd = 4, col = "blue") abline(0, 0) abline(v = 0) legend("topleft", legend = c("New generalized Wiener process", "Original generalized Wiener process", "Drift" , "New stochastic component", "Original stochastic component"), lty = 1, bg = "white", lwd = 2, col = c("black", "grey", "blue", "red", "grey"), cex = 0.75) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Generalized Wiener process, higher b.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-gwphb-1.png){#fig-gwphb width=672} ::: ::: Note the changes are now more pronounced. This is a replication of @Hull, table 14.1. Simulation of stock price when $\mu= 0.15$ and $\sigma = 0.30$ during 1-week periods. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Table 14.1 set.seed(19256) delta_S <- rep(0, 10) S <- rep(100, 10) epsilon <- rep(0, 10) for(i in 1:10){ epsilon[i] <- rnorm(1, 0, 1) delta_S[i] <- 0.15 * (1 / 52) * S[i] + 0.3 * ((1 / 52)^0.5) * epsilon[i] * S[i] S[i+1] <- S[i] + delta_S[i] } epsilon <- c(epsilon, NA) delta_S <- c(delta_S, NA) results <- data.frame(S, epsilon, delta_S) results ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` S epsilon delta_S 1 100.0000 0.8268625 3.7284174 2 103.7284 0.4957988 2.4387684 3 106.1672 -0.9313823 -3.8074983 4 102.3597 -0.1495925 -0.3417592 5 102.0179 0.7310239 3.3968957 6 105.4148 -0.6111472 -2.3761181 7 103.0387 1.2490868 5.6516490 8 108.6904 -0.6243389 -2.5096009 9 106.1808 0.6995349 3.3964067 10 109.5772 0.3612549 1.9629353 11 111.5401 NA NA ``` ::: ::: It is interesting to note that in @Hull the final simulated price is \$111.54 and here it is \$111.5401. Is this a pure coincidence? A correlated process. $\Delta x_1 = a_1 \Delta t + b_1 \epsilon_1 \sqrt{\Delta t}$, $\Delta x_2 = a_2 \Delta t + b_2 \epsilon_2 \sqrt{\Delta t}$. Where: $\epsilon_1 = u$, and $\epsilon_2=\rho u + \sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}v$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Section 14.5 rho <- 0.8 TT <- 1 steps <- 1000 dt <- TT / steps Time <- seq(from = dt, to = TT, by = dt) set.seed(123) e1 <- rnorm(steps, 0, 1) e2 <- rho * e1 + ((1 - rho^2)^0.5) * rnorm(steps, 0, 1) delta_z1 <- 1.5 * e1 * dt^0.5 delta_z2 <- 1.5 * e2 * dt^0.5 delta_x1 <- (0.3 * dt) + delta_z1 delta_x2 <- (0.3 * dt) + delta_z2 x1 <- cumsum(delta_x1) x2 <- cumsum(delta_x2) x <- c(x1, x2) ``` ::: Assume $a=0.3$, $b=1.5$ and $\rho=0.8$. Visually: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(Time, x1, type = "l", ylim = c(min(x), max(x)), ylab = "x1 and x2") lines(Time, x2, col = "red") abline(0, 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Correlated processes x1 and x2.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-cpxax-1.png){#fig-cpxax width=672} ::: ::: The model of stock price behavior implies that a stock's price at time $T$, given its price today, is lognormally distributed. The following is similar to example 15.1 in @Hull. In particular, consider a stock with an initial price of \$40, an expected return of 16% per annum, and a volatility of 20% per annum. $\frac{\Delta S}{S} = a \Delta t + b \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t}$, $\frac{\Delta S}{S} = 0.16 \Delta t + 0.2 \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t}$, Assume $\Delta t = \frac{1}{365}$: $\frac{\Delta S}{S} = 0.16 \frac{1}{365} + 0.2 \epsilon \sqrt{\frac{1}{365}}$, Calculate the constants: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} 0.16/365 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.0004383562 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} 0.2*(1/365)^0.5 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.01046848 ``` ::: ::: Finally: $\Delta S = 0.0004383562 S + 0.01046848 S \epsilon$. The 100 simulated 6-months paths of the stock price $S$ are: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(10101010) for(j in 1:100) { delta_S <- NULL S <- rep(40, 180) # Now it is 40 for all days, the loop fill this out. epsilon <- NULL for(i in 1:180) { epsilon[i] <- rnorm(1, 0, 1) delta_S[i] <- 0.16 * (1 / 365) * S[i] + 0.2 * ((1 / 365)^0.5) * epsilon[i] * S[i] S[i+1] <- S[i] + delta_S[i] } if (j==1) { plot(S, type = "l", ylim = c(30, 60), ylab = expression(paste(S[t])), xlab = "t (days)") points(0, 40, pch = 19, col = "blue", cex = 3) abline(h = 10, col = "red", lwd = 3) } lines(S) } abline(h = 32.55, lwd = 2, col = "red") abline(h = 56.56, lwd = 2, col = "red") abline(v = 181, lwd = 2, col = "red") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A geometric Brownian motion simulation: 100 stock price paths.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-agbms1spp-1.png){#fig-agbms1spp width=672} ::: ::: The code above can be problematic because we are not collecting all paths in an object. An alternative code is the following. Again, this is similar to example 15.1 in @Hull. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} S0 <- 40 nDays <- 360 mu <- 0.16 sig <- 0.2 TT <- 0.5 mean.logST <- log(S0)+(mu - (sig^2 / 2)) * TT variance.logST <- sig^2 * TT lower.ST <- exp(mean.logST + qnorm(0.025) * variance.logST^0.5) upper.ST <- exp(mean.logST + qnorm(0.975) * variance.logST^0.5) set.seed(3) nSim <- 1000 nSteps <- nDays * TT SP_sim <- matrix(0, nrow = nSteps + 1, ncol = nSim) for(i in 1:nSim){ SVec <- rep(0, nSteps + 1) SVec[1] <- S0 for(j in 2:(nSteps + 1)){ DeltaS <- mu * SVec[j - 1] * (1 / nDays) + sig * SVec[j - 1] * rnorm(1) * (1 / nDays)^0.5 SVec[j] <- SVec[j - 1] + DeltaS } SP_sim[, i] <- SVec } ``` ::: Now, let's plot the results. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} matplot(0:nSteps, SP_sim, type = 'l', col = rgb(0, 0, 1 ,0.3), lty = 1, ylab = expression(paste(S[t])), xlab = "Time") points(0, S0, pch = 19, cex = 3) abline(h = lower.ST) abline(h = upper.ST) abline(v = nSteps) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A geometric Brownian motion simulation: 1000 stock price paths.](03-wiener-processes_files/figure-html/fig-agbms1spp2-1.png){#fig-agbms1spp2 width=672} ::: ::: Let's verify that there is approximately a 95% probability that the stock price in 6 months will lie between the lower and upper bounds shown above. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} 1 - (sum(SP_sim[nSteps + 1, ] < lower.ST) + sum(SP_sim[nSteps + 1, ] > upper.ST)) / 1000 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.967 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} S0 <- 40 mu <- 0.16 sig <- 0.2 TT <- 0.5 mean.logST <- log(S0)+(mu - (sig^2 / 2)) * TT variance.logST <- sig^2 * TT lower.ST <- exp(mean.logST + qnorm(0.025) * variance.logST^0.5) upper.ST <- exp(mean.logST + qnorm(0.975) * variance.logST^0.5) lower.ST ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 32.51491 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} upper.ST ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 56.6029 ``` ::: ::: Nice. This is how a stock price evolves according to the Black-Scholes formula.
